questions.context: Gravity is the universal force of attraction that draws any two objects with mass toward each other. Although it is the weakest of nature's fundamental forces, it governs the motion of planets, the falling of objects, and the structure of galaxies. Its strength depends on two quantities: the masses of the objects involved and the distance separating them, growing weaker as that distance increases. [■] This dependence makes gravity a force whose reach extends across the entire cosmos. [■] The way gravity weakens with distance follows a precise mathematical relationship known as the inverse-square law. Doubling the separation between two bodies reduces the gravitational pull to a quarter of its former value. [■] Near a planet's surface, by contrast, the distance changes so little that gravity feels nearly constant, pulling everything downward at a steady rate. [■] However, this familiar picture confronts limitations. At extreme scales, such as near black holes or across vast cosmic distances, the simple Newtonian description begins to fail, and a more complete theory of gravity becomes necessary to explain the observed motions accurately.

questions.context: Transpiration is the process by which plants release water vapor into the atmosphere through tiny pores on their leaves. This continuous loss of water, far from being merely wasteful, drives the upward movement of water and dissolved minerals from the roots to the highest leaves. The pores responsible, called stomata, open and close in response to environmental conditions such as light, humidity, and temperature. [■] When stomata open to admit the carbon dioxide needed for photosynthesis, water inevitably escapes alongside it. [■] The escaping vapor creates a pull, or tension, that is transmitted down continuous columns of water inside narrow vessels in the stem. [■] Because water molecules cling tightly to one another, this tension lifts an unbroken chain of water from the soil to the canopy, sometimes raising it many meters against the force of gravity. [■] This mechanism allows even the tallest trees to supply their leaves without any pump. However, the system has a cost. During droughts, excessive water loss can cause the columns to break, introducing air bubbles that block flow and may ultimately threaten the plant's survival.

questions.context: A gnomon is a simple upright rod or pillar whose shadow, cast by the sun, was used by ancient peoples to track time and the seasons. By marking the length and direction of its shadow throughout the day, early observers could identify noon, when the shadow was shortest, and orient themselves with respect to north and south. The gnomon thus served as both clock and compass long before mechanical instruments existed. [■] The shadow's behavior also revealed the slow rhythm of the year. Across the seasons, the noon shadow lengthened and shortened in a regular cycle, reaching its extremes at the solstices. [■] By recording these turning points, astronomers in Egypt, Greece, and China fixed the dates of the longest and shortest days with surprising accuracy. [■] From such measurements they even estimated the tilt of the Earth's axis, a quantity central to later models of the heavens. [■] The significance of the gnomon therefore extended well beyond timekeeping into the foundations of mathematical astronomy. However, its readings depended on clear skies and careful observation, and small errors in measurement could accumulate, limiting the precision that any single observer could achieve.

options.B: averages

options.D: dangers

questions.context: Population aging is the steady rise in the share of older people within a society, driven by longer life spans and falling birth rates. In many countries, the number of retirees is growing far faster than the number of working-age adults who support them. This imbalance strains pension systems and health services, since fewer workers must fund the needs of a larger dependent group. Without adjustment, the gap between contributions and obligations widens each year. One widely adopted response is to encourage longer working lives. Governments have gradually raised the official retirement age and removed penalties that once discouraged employment past a fixed point. Employers, in turn, have introduced flexible schedules and retraining programs designed to keep experienced staff productive. By extending careers, these measures enlarge the pool of contributors while reducing the years of support each retiree requires. The results have been mixed but encouraging. In several nations, employment among people over sixty has risen markedly, easing pressure on public budgets. However, the approach has limits. Physically demanding jobs cannot always accommodate older workers, and unequal health across the population means that some groups benefit far less than others from a later retirement.

questions.context: Monopsony describes a labor market in which a single dominant employer, rather than many competing firms, controls the demand for workers in a region or occupation. Because workers have few alternative places to sell their labor, the dominant firm can hold wages below the level that open competition would produce. This power persists even when many people seek jobs, since switching employers may require relocation or abandoning specialized skills. The result is a quiet but persistent suppression of pay. Policymakers have proposed several remedies for this imbalance. Minimum wage laws set a floor beneath which pay cannot fall, directly countering the employer's leverage. Stronger collective bargaining lets workers negotiate as a group, restoring some of the balance that competition would normally supply. Restrictions on non-compete clauses, meanwhile, make it easier for employees to move between firms. The evidence on these measures is encouraging but incomplete. Studies suggest that modest wage floors in concentrated markets can raise pay without reducing employment, contrary to older predictions. However, the effects vary widely across industries. Where a single firm dominates an entire town, even well-designed policies may struggle to overcome the structural advantage that the employer enjoys.

questions.subtopic: how urban heat islands affect local ecosystems
questions.context: Walk from a shaded city park into a downtown parking lot on a summer afternoon, and you'll feel the temperature jump several degrees in just a few steps. That gap has a name. Ecologists call it the urban heat island effect, and it describes how cities trap and re-radiate heat far more than the countryside around them. Dark asphalt and concrete absorb solar energy during the day and release it slowly at night, so a city can stay warm long after sunset while nearby farmland cools off. Now, the interesting part for an ecologist is not the discomfort, it's what this does to living communities. Consider insects. A research team tracking aphids and the ladybugs that prey on them found something unexpected in warmer city blocks. The aphids reproduced faster in the heat, but the ladybugs, which are more sensitive to temperature, became sluggish and ate less. So the predators couldn't keep up, and aphid outbreaks spread through urban gardens. The same warming also shifts the timing of biological events. Flowers in heat-island neighborhoods bloomed nearly two weeks earlier than the same species in cooler suburbs. That sounds harmless, but the pollinators that depend on those flowers don't always arrive early too. When the bloom and the bee fall out of step, both sides lose. So the heat island isn't just an engineering problem about hot pavement. It quietly rewires predator-prey balances and the seasonal clockwork that holds local ecosystems together, and it offers ecologists a kind of natural laboratory for the warming the whole planet is heading toward.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How warmer city temperatures disrupt local ecological relationships
options.B: Why asphalt and concrete absorb more heat than farmland
options.C: How cities can be redesigned to lower summer temperatures
options.D: The discomfort people feel walking through hot urban areas

questions.subtopic: how mirror neurons relate to empathy and imitation
questions.context: Have you ever winced when you watched someone else stub their toe? You weren't hurt, yet your brain reacted almost as if you were. That reaction points us toward today's topic: mirror neurons. A mirror neuron is a brain cell that fires both when you perform an action and when you simply watch someone else perform that same action. The discovery happened almost by accident. In the early 1990s, researchers in Italy were recording from the brains of macaque monkeys, studying cells that controlled hand movements. They noticed that certain neurons fired when a monkey picked up a peanut. But here's the surprise. The very same neurons also fired when the monkey just watched a researcher pick up a peanut. The monkey wasn't moving at all. Its brain was, in a sense, mirroring the action it saw. Scientists think this mirroring helps explain two very human abilities. The first is imitation. Watching someone tie a knot activates the same circuits you'd use to tie it yourself, which makes copying the motion easier to learn. The second is empathy. When you see a friend smile or flinch, mirror systems may let you internally simulate that feeling, so you understand it from the inside rather than just observing it. Of course, mirror neurons aren't the whole story of empathy, and scientists still debate exactly how much they explain. But the core idea is striking. Part of how we connect with other people may come from brain cells that blur the line between doing and watching.

options.A: How monkeys learn to pick up food in laboratory studies
options.B: Brain cells that fire when we act and when we watch
options.C: Why some people feel more empathy than others do
options.D: How researchers record electrical signals inside the brain

The monkey wasn't moving at all. Its brain was, in a sense, mirroring the action it saw. Scientists think this mirroring helps explain two very human abilities. The first is imitation. Watching someone tie a knot activates the same circuits you'd use to tie it yourself, which makes copying the motion easier to learn. The second is empathy. When you see a friend smile or flinch, mirror systems may let you internally simulate that feeling, so you understand it from the inside rather than just observing it. Of course, mirror neurons aren't the whole story of empathy, and scientists still debate exactly how much they explain. But the core idea is striking. Part of how we connect with other people may come from brain cells that blur the line between doing and watching.

options.D: They activate the same circuits used to perform the watched action

questions.context: Imagine an animal you can boil, freeze near absolute zero, blast with radiation, or even launch into the vacuum of space, and it just shrugs it off. That animal exists, and it's smaller than a grain of sand. We call it the tardigrade, or sometimes the water bear. Tardigrades are tiny eight-legged creatures that live in moss, soil, and water all over the planet. What makes them famous is their ability to survive conditions that would kill almost anything else. The secret isn't toughness in the ordinary sense. It's a strategy called cryptobiosis. When their environment dries out, tardigrades pull in their legs, expel most of the water from their bodies, and curl into a shriveled ball known as a tun. In this state, their metabolism nearly stops. They aren't quite dead, but they aren't really living either, just suspended. Now, how do they avoid falling apart without water? Researchers discovered they produce special proteins that, as the body dries, turn the cell's interior into a kind of glass. This glassy state locks delicate structures in place so they don't collapse or break. When water returns, sometimes years later, the glass dissolves, the tardigrade rehydrates, and it walks away as if nothing happened. This matters beyond curiosity. Scientists are studying those proteins to find better ways to preserve vaccines and other fragile biological materials without refrigeration. So a creature you can barely see may end up teaching us how to keep medicines stable in the harshest places on Earth.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Where tardigrades can be found across different habitats
options.B: How scientists store vaccines without using refrigeration
options.C: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions through cryptobiosis
options.D: Why tardigrades are smaller than a single grain of sand

questions.context: Suppose you're convinced a new coworker is unfriendly. From that moment on, every time they skip a greeting, you think, see, I knew it. But the days they smile and say good morning? Those somehow slip right past you. That lopsided way of noticing is today's subject: confirmation bias. Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, favor, and remember information that supports what we already believe, while downplaying evidence that contradicts it. It isn't lying to ourselves on purpose. It's a quiet filter running in the background of ordinary thinking. A classic study makes this vivid. Researchers gave people a number sequence, two, four, six, and asked them to guess the rule behind it. Most decided the rule was even numbers counting up, and then they only tested examples that fit that guess, like eight, ten, twelve. They kept getting yes for an answer, so they felt confident. But the actual rule was simply any increasing numbers. Because they only tested cases meant to confirm their idea, they never tried something like one, two, three, which would have revealed the truth. Confirmation bias matters far beyond puzzles. A doctor who settles on a diagnosis early may notice symptoms that fit it and overlook ones that don't. An investor convinced a stock will rise may read only the rosy forecasts. The danger isn't that we have beliefs. It's that we rarely go looking for the evidence that could prove us wrong. And recognizing that tendency is the first step toward thinking more carefully.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why first impressions of coworkers are usually accurate
options.B: How doctors and investors are trained to make decisions
options.C: A famous number puzzle used in psychology classes
options.D: A mental tendency to favor evidence that fits our beliefs

For questions, email workstudy@campus.edu or stop by during walk-in hours, Monday through Thursday, 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM.

Best regards,
Daniel Okafor
Financial Aid Coordinator

questions.subtopic: two students comparing notes after a guest lecture on ocean currents
questions.context:
Woman: Did you catch everything in that guest lecture on ocean currents? My notes are a mess.
Man: Mostly. The part about how wind drives the surface currents made sense, but the deep-water section lost me.
Woman: Same here. Apparently temperature and salt levels pull the colder water downward.
Man: Right, and that's what keeps the whole system circulating. The professor called it a conveyor belt.
Woman: That image actually helps. Want to combine our notes before the quiz on Thursday?
Man: Definitely. Let's meet at the library tomorrow afternoon.

questions.subtopic: a student asking a librarian how to open an online journal from off campus
questions.context:
Man: Excuse me, I'm trying to open a journal for my history paper, but the site keeps asking me to pay.
Woman: Are you connecting from off campus? The library subscription only works through our own system.
Man: I'm at my apartment, yeah. So I can't read it from home at all?
Woman: You can, but you have to log in through the library's proxy link first, using your student ID.
Man: Oh, I didn't even know that existed. Where do I find the link?
Woman: It's on the database page, top right corner. Bookmark it for next time.
questions.stem: Why can't the man open the journal from home?

options.A: His student account has already expired
options.B: The journal is no longer subscribed to
options.C: He is not connecting through the library system
options.D: The database is down for maintenance
